text: The surgeon begins the total laparoscopic hysterectomy by cutting the round ligament using an ultrasonic scalpel, taking care to avoid the artery below. The fallopian tube is then treated and cut with the harmonic scalpel. Bipolar energy is used to ensure hemostasis for the uterine ovarian pedicle before safely cutting the ligaments. The adnexal vein is visible just above the ovary.

The surgeon coagulates the adnexal vein to facilitate opening the broad ligament. The uterus is manipulated with a uterine manipulator, while the assistant provides cranial traction on the bladder. The dissection proceeds from one round ligament to the other, aiming for a dry, bloodless dissection. The bladder is dissected after cutting both round ligaments to enhance uterine mobility, with small bleeders controlled for safe operation.

The surgeon pushes the uterus to the left side of the patient and performs small hemostasis on the connective tissue to increase uterine mobility. The right round ligament is cut using a harmonic scalpel, with careful control of the artery located just below the ligament. The surgeon uses the ultrasonic scalpel to control the tube, while the utero-ovarian ligament requires prior control with bipolar energy due to larger vessels.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to safely coagulate the utero-ovarian pedicle while pushing the uterus medially. A corpus luteum is visible on the right ovary. Care is taken to avoid burning the bowel located just below. The surgeon then switches to the harmonic scalpel to further control the utero-ovarian pedicle at the site of previous bipolar coagulation.

The surgeon pushes the uterus medially to access the utero-ovarian ligament, coagulating it before cutting. The right adnexal vein is visible, requiring bipolar coagulation for control before using the harmonic scalpel to cut, ensuring safe access to the uterosacral ligaments.

The surgeon cuts the left adnexal vein and proceeds medially to increase uterine mobility. The posterior part of the broad ligament is opened, and the peritoneum is cut to expose the uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels, identifying the uterine artery and vein. Dissection is performed between the uterine artery and the peritoneum to enhance uterine mobility. The bipolar tool is used for dissection near the upper part of the left uterosacral ligament. The surgeon proceeds with the vesico-uterine dissection, opening the Halman plane using the harmonic scalpel, while carefully avoiding damage to the uterine vessels near the medial part of the bladder pillar.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to safely dissect the vagico-vaginal plane, ensuring the small vessel is coagulated before proceeding with the bladder dissection. The scalpel's cold part is utilized to avoid burning the tissue, increasing the space between the bladder and the vagina. The medial part of the bladder pillar is visible during this process.

The surgeon performs vascular control on small vessels with varicosities near the medial part of the bladder pilar. The upper part of the bladder pilar is cut, avoiding risk to the ureter and nerves. The uterine artery and vein are visible, requiring careful dissection to prevent damage. The uterine manipulator is positioned, indicating sufficient space for vaginal closure.

The surgeon removes connective tissue surrounding the uterine pedicle to enhance coagulation effectiveness. The forceps are applied with energy until no bubbles are visible, indicating the tissue is dry and can be safely cut without hemorrhage. The vessel is coagulated over two to three centimeters to minimize bleeding from the remaining uterus.

The surgeon controls the cervical vaginal vessel on the left side using coagulation, ensuring no bubbles are present to indicate complete hemostasis. The surrounding tissue is managed, and the pedicle is controlled over a span of two to three centimeters. The surgeon then moves to the right side of the uterus, cutting through the connective tissue to expose the right uterine artery.

The surgeon carefully approaches the right uterine pedicle, performing hemostasis to prevent bleeding. A small section of tissue is cut to improve access to the uterine artery, and the surrounding peritoneum is removed to isolate the artery. Short bursts of coagulation are applied until bubbles between the forceps arms disappear, indicating effective coagulation.

The surgeon efficiently controls the right uterine artery, moving up and down the isthmus while lateralizing the uterus to increase the distance from the pedicle. The uterine artery is cut, followed by the uterine vein, positioned between the arms of the bipolar forceps. The cervical vaginal pedicle is then controlled and cut before proceeding with the colpotomy using a hook.

The surgeon carefully removes the bladder from the dissection area to minimize the risk of thermal injury. Using a uterine manipulator and a hook, the surgeon performs a colpotomy, cutting from right to left across the uterine manipulator. The incision is made laterally, targeting the posterior part of the vagina just above the uterosacral ligaments.

The surgeon extracts a large uterus with a ten-centimeter myoma through the vagina. To close the vaginal opening, a Vicryl Zero suture with a fourteen-millimeter needle is used, starting at the lateral part of the vagina. The needle is inserted into the mucosa to prevent post-operative bleeding, incorporating the peritoneum into the stitch. An extracorporeal knotting technique with a rudder knot is employed, which locks itself. The same procedure is repeated on the opposite side, securing any bleeders with the stitch and performing an interrupted suture.

The surgeon applies pressure with a stitch to stop the bleeding and proceeds to close the medial part of the vagina. Care is taken to avoid including the bladder in the stitch by directing the needle vector appropriately. The needle is inserted into the peritoneum during the suture.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal knotting, creating two flat knots in the same position and one knot on the opposite side. The stitch is placed through the anterior and posterior parts of the vagina, incorporating the peritoneum.

The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using wire knotting tape, securing the final knot to ensure the closure is complete. The right ovary is observed to be highly mobile, prompting the surgeon to perform an ovariopexy to stabilize the ovary and reduce the risk of ovariitis.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy on the right ovary, securing it to reduce mobility and prevent torsion. The round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary are included in the suture. The same procedure is repeated on the opposite side, with the knot being locked to complete the interruption.
The surgeon inspects the pelvis, confirming that the ovary is secured and cannot undergo torsion. The ureter is visible and positioned away from the working area, with no signs of bleeding.
